zákon o azylu.reason: The alias "zákon o azylu" directly and explicitly matches the official short title of law 325/1999 Sb., which is "Zákon o azylu". Although 283/1991 Sb. has a higher frequency, its official name "Zákon České národní rady o Policii České republiky" is unrelated to asylum. The other candidates are either unrelated or are amending laws, not the primary law on asylum itself. Therefore, 325/1999 Sb. is the canonical law for this alias.

občanský zákoník.reason: The alias "občanský zákoník" (Civil Code) in contemporary Czech legal discourse almost exclusively refers to the current Civil Code, Act No. 89/2012 Sb., which came into effect on January 1, 2014. While Act No. 40/1964 Sb. was the previous Civil Code, it is no longer in force and would only be referenced in historical contexts or for specific transitional provisions. For a modern, canonical reference, 89/2012 Sb. is the clear choice.

s. ř..reason: The abbreviation "s. ř." stands for "správní řád" (Administrative Procedure Code). While 71/1967 Sb. was an older Administrative Procedure Code, it has been repealed. In modern Czech legal discourse, when "správní řád" or its abbreviation "s. ř." is used without further specification, it refers to the currently valid Administrative Procedure Code, which is 500/2004 Sb. Therefore, 500/2004 Sb. is the indisputable canonical modern law represented by this alias.

zákon o odpovědnosti státu.reason: The alias "zákon o odpovědnosti státu" (Law on state liability) directly and unambiguously refers to Act No. 82/1998 Sb., which is titled "Zákon o odpovědnosti za škodu způsobenou při výkonu veřejné moci rozhodnutím nebo nesprávným úředním postupem...". This law is the primary legal regulation governing state liability in the Czech Republic. Act No. 358/1992 Sb. is about notaries and their activities, and its inclusion as a candidate is likely due to the fact that Act No. 82/1998 Sb. also amended it, leading to a spurious association. However, the alias itself does not refer to the Notarial Code. Therefore, 82/1998 Sb. is the indisputable canonical law.

daňový řád.reason: The alias "daňový řád" directly and unequivocally refers to Law ID 280/2009 Sb., which is officially named "Zákon daňový řád". This is the current and canonical tax code in the Czech Republic. While 337/1992 Sb. was a predecessor law concerning tax administration, it is not officially called "daňový řád" and is no longer in force as the primary tax code. The other candidates are clearly not laws or are unrelated to the alias.

zákoník práce.reason: The alias "zákoník práce" (Labour Code) refers to the current Labour Code in the Czech Republic. While 65/1965 Sb. was an older Labour Code, 262/2006 Sb. is the modern and currently effective Labour Code. Following the instruction to choose the indisputable canonical, modern law, 262/2006 Sb. is the correct choice, similar to how 'občanský zákoník' refers to 89/2012 Sb. and not the older 40/1964 Sb.

zákon o zaměstnanosti.reason: The alias "zákon o zaměstnanosti" directly and unambiguously refers to Law No. 435/2004 Sb., which is officially titled "Zákon o zaměstnanosti". This is the current and canonical law on employment in the Czech Republic. Law No. 9/1991 Sb. is an older, superseded regulation, and Law No. 261/2021 Sb. is an amending law, not the primary employment act itself.

zákon o státní kontrole.reason: The alias "zákon o státní kontrole" directly and unambiguously refers to Law ID 552/1991 Sb., which is officially named "Zákon České národní rady o státní kontrole". The other candidate, 230/2006 Sb., is an amending law primarily concerning the state statistical service, not state control, and therefore does not match the alias.

katastrální zákon.reason: The alias "katastrální zákon" explicitly appears in the official names of both candidate laws. However, 256/2013 Sb. is the current and modern law governing the real estate cadastre in the Czech Republic, having superseded the older 344/1992 Sb. When referred to without further specification in contemporary legal texts, "katastrální zákon" unambiguously refers to the currently valid law, which is 256/2013 Sb.
